texts.full_text: e gas with strong spin orbit interactions. We argue that it is an intrinsic property, in the sense that it is independent of the strength of disorder scattering. We show numerically that the spin polarization near the edge induced by this mechanism can be large, and that it becomes larger and more strongly localized as the spin-orbit coupling strength increases, and is independent of the width of the conductchannel. The results are significant for understanding how to generate robust localized magnetic states in semiconductor devices through intrinsic mechanisms rather than extrinsic ones such as doping or defect engineering. Our findings suggest that by controlling the strength of spin-orbit interactions, one can manipulate edge-localized spins with high precision and stability. This opens up new possibilities for designing efficient quantum bits (qubits) based on hole carriers and potentially enabling novel types of spintronic devices where localized magnetic moments play a central role. Our simulations were performed using state-of-the-art computational techniques that accurately capture the effects of strong spin-orbit coupling in two-dimensional systems, providing insights into fundamental transport phenomena governed by relativistic quantum mechanics. texts.full_text: colitis (UC) have not been elucidated and nostudies have compared between UC and Crohn's disease (CD). The objective of this study was to investigate the small bowel lesions in UC, and to characterize UC lesionsby comparison with CD. Subjects comprised 54 UC patients and39CD patients whounderwent CE. We retrospectively investigated characteristics ofsmall bowel lesions in UC. We also compared the findings between UC and CD groups. ### Methods - Patients: A total of 54 patients diagnosed with ulcerative colitis (UC) and 39 patients diagnosed with Crohn's disease (CD), all of whom underwent capsule endoscopy, were included in the study. - Capsule Endoscopy Procedure: All subjects had a standard CE procedure performedusing an M2A PillCam® or similar device. The videos were reviewedby experiencedgastroenterologists blinded to patient diagnosis for assessment of small bowel lesions. - Lesion Assessment: - Lesions such as erosions, ulcers, strictures, and inflammatory changes in the mucosa were documented. - Severity was graded based on established criteria (e.g., Boston Bowel Preparation Scaleor similar).- Statistical Analysis: Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics. texts.full_text: By a fortuitous coincidence, I had the opportunity to see two consecutive presentations from search engine companies banking on natural language processing (NLP) to power the next generation of search. The first was from Ron Kaplan, Chief Technology and Science Officer of Powerset, who presented at Colusure in San Francisco last week about "The Search for Meaning." He spoke eloquently but I found myself distracted by how much his talk had to do with the problem domain he addressed: information retrieval (IR). The second was from Adam Geitgey, a Google research scientist who presented at an internal seminar on natural language understanding and semantic parsing. His work is part of Project MUMBLE - Machine Understanding Through Big Learning Engines. My purpose here isn't to recap either talk but rather to address the broader issue: how much does NLP have in common with IR? I'm going back over some familiar territory, as this has been an area of debate for years. The answer is that there's a lot more overlap than most people realize and less separation between these two fields. There are many ways you could try to define information retrieval or natural language processing (and probably countless arguments about what those definitions should be). Cheetah was designed by Boston-based engineering firm Boston Dynamics Inc., under DARPA funding. The company also built BigDog , another four-legged robot that can haul heavy loads over rugged terrain and has been tested in Afghanistan . DARPA is developing Cheetah as part of a larger effort called the Maximum Mobility and Manipulation program to build legged robots capable of navigating rough or uneven outdoor environments. The cheetah runs at 28.3 miles per hour (mph) on a treadmill, setting an unofficial speed record for four-legged runners. "We wanted something that was fast enough and light-weight," said Dr Darryn Reid from the US military's Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA). He added: "We have other programs which are developing exoskeletons to carry loads but we're interested in what a robot can do when it doesn't need to be carried by someone else." The cheetah will eventually run off-road, carrying an array of sensors and cameras. "It is being designed for military applications," said Dr Reid. "It's not meant just as something that could go out on your driveway but perhaps in the field or even deployed somewhere where it would need to operate autonomously". He added: "We want this thing running through rough terrain, up hill and down dale so we're looking at a number of different terrains." Cheetah's 28.3 mph speed compares with Usain Bolt 's world record run for the men's 100m dash in South Korea last year - he ran it in just over nine seconds. The cheetah is built from strong, light-weight carbon fibre to keep its weight down so that the animal can move quickly and efficiently on any surface. texts.full_text: drama with high-stakes action, and Blunt’s performance anchors both. Her deadpan wit (“I’m not -fire, I’m -not-getting-burned”) and the sheer physicality of Elastigirl’s superheroics (stretching, slapping, and smacking villains with equal flair) make her the emotional and literal backbone of the film. While the first leaned into the nostalgia of 60s-era superhero design, the sequel doubles down on the family saga. Bob/Mr. Incredible’s dad-flu-inspired incompetence and Dash’s teenaged eye-rolls feel ripped from any millennial’s family chat, but it’s Violet’s coming-of-age arc—balancing her powers, her crush on Frozone, and her role as the “invisible” daughter—that adds heft. The villain? A twist on the classic “rogue inventor” trope, but with a meta-nod to animation itself (more on that later). What truly elevates is its geeky attention to detail. The mid-credits sequence? A loving callback to ’ original post-credits gag, but with a twist that’ll make Pixar purists cheer. And let’s not forget the animation—those stretchy, rubber-hose physics of Elastigirl’s limbs? A technical marvel that feels like a love letter to classic cartoons. In the end, it’s a film that understands geeks and parents alike: Sometimes the real superpower isn’t flying or invisibility, but surviving a family road trip with the AC broken and Dash’s math

texts.full_text: is possible, however, to implement optimal *imperfect* conclusive teleportation. As we shall see in this section, this is achieved when Alice applies MC measurements [@Croke06; @Jimenez11] to discriminate the states $|\nu_l\rangle_2$. With such strategy, conclusive events in the discrimination process occur with maximum success probability and lead to a teleportation with the maximum achievable fidelity for the quantum channel. We show that this fidelity depends only on the Schmidt rank of the channel and, although less than a unit, it is larger than the one achieved deterministically \[see Eq. (\[eq:F\_opt\])\]. In the following we shall, first, briefly review MC discrimination for symmetric states [@Jimenez11], and then apply this strategy to the teleportation process. The advantages as well as the drawbacks of the protocol are discussed and an example is presented to illustrate it. It is worth mentioning that the problem addressed here could equivalently be thought as the teleportation of $D$-dimensional qudit states using two entangled $N$-dimensional qudits. In fact, this problem has been discussed in Ref. [@Laustsen02] from a completely different approach and restricted to maximally entangled states only. In our protocol, however, this is just a special case that occurs when all the Schmidt coefficients in (\[QUANTUMCHANNEL\]) are equal as will be shown later. texts.full_text: the basis of the artist’s lyrics. The scholars also show powerful evidence of a gender effect in music preferences. They write: The present article also provides the first evidence that listeners tend to prefer music from artists of the same sex, which had been previously theorized but not studied empirically (Hagen & Bryant, 2003). That is, the gender-fit between the listener and artist was found to be a significant predictor of musical preferences. So, the next time you say yourself, "I love that artist's music." Ask yourself, what do you know about that performer? Why do you like that artist? For companies, of course, the implications are profound. Can they use this finding to refine their algorithms, without intruding on our personal privacy? What if listeners were willing to disclose more about their personality in order to discover more music they would enjoy? texts.full_text: Impact Protein Adsorption and Cell Adhesion. Controlling the reversibility, quantity, and extent of biomolecule interaction at interfaces has a significant relevance for biomedical and biotechnological applications, because protein adsorption is always the first step when a solid surface gets in contact with a biological fluid. Polymer brushes, composed of end-tethered linear Polymers with sufficient grafting density, are very promising to control these interactions due to their unique architecture that can modulate surface properties on demand. Thermoresponsive polymer brushes offer an exceptional way to control protein adsorption and cell adhesion by exploiting temperature-induced phase transitions. These materials undergo a change in hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity at specific temperatures, which allows for the reversible modulation of biomolecule interactions without altering their chemical composition fundamentally. The nanoarchitectonics aspect refers to precisely designing these polymer brushes on the nanoscale level to achieve desired properties and functionalities. By tuning parameters such as grafting density, chain length, monomer type, and temperature response characteristics, researchers can optimize surfaces for various applications in drug delivery systems, tissue engineering, biosensors, and more. For instance, increasing the grafting density of thermoresponsive polymer brushes enhances their ability to resist non-specific protein adsorption even at higher temperatures. Similarly, varying chain lengths or incorporating specific functional groups into the polymers allows fine-tuning of cell

texts.full_text: You think that it’s interesting that every piece of music recorded ever is on Spotify. It’s not nearly the case. So we’re still recording from from our physical archives. Such massive amounts of music require mindful organization, and in this conversation we delved into how such a thing can be structured to make particular pieces of music easier to find. In our case, our data model basically supports two types of composition. And one is, you could say, the normal type of composition where you have a title for the composition and then you would have composers and lyricists related to that. And the other type of composition would support sub-compositions, which is basically in one of the obvious example is you have a symphony which would have four movements and then and so those are the sub-compositions. And we are then able to relate each of these sub-compositions or movements to all the different recordings of this movement and this work. We also discussed a problem I’ve had with my own music collection: how to organize pieces that originated before the era of recording technologies, and which don’t fit neatly into album-length containers. The show is worth your time — especially if you manage a lot of music. The Informed Life Episode 18: thomas Dose on Music Collections Related Posted in Blog MediaPodc

texts.full_text: driver. The motor control part sets a target current value for a motor current passing through the motor on the basis of the steering torque signal and the vehicle speed signal, obtains the difference between a signal pertaining to this target current value (a target current signal) and a motor current signal fed back from a motor current detecting part for detecting the motor current actually flowing through the motor, and performs proportional/integral compensation processing (PI control) on thisdifference in order that an assist torque corresponding with the auxiliary steering force to be produced by means of assistance operation is outputted. In contrast thereto, steer-by-wire systems are completely devoided [deprived] from any mechanical linkage between a vehicle's controls (steering wheel) and actual wheels being steered on ground level; instead these signals for commands regarding the turning direction or angle by which to turn is directly sent through electronic means. The steering force generated in such steer-by-wire systems can be entirely derived [generated] from an electric motor controlled through computer algorithms, thereby providing more flexibility as well fine-tuned control over how much resistance should accompany any given amount of rotation at the wheel. Regardless if using electrical power-steering assistance or full-on wire-controlled steering solutions both these systems have played a significant role in improving driving comfort and convenience while simultaneously improving car's maneuverability [enhancing] under various circumstances. In terms for safety aspects, integration with driver-assistance systems like lane keeping assist (LKA), adaptive cruise control etc., can play significant part by further increasing the risk of crashes or accidents especially during long hours on road. It is also evident that these modern-day technologies are heavily dependent on sophisticated sensors and actuators to function effectively. As they can constantly interpret steering inputs, vehicle speed data [readings], wheel positions etc., which requires high accuracy along with robustness against any potential interference or degradation in performance over time due course of usage. An exposure unit then creates an electrostatic latent image on this charged surface by selectively discharging areas according to digital data representing the desired print output, typically achieved through laser scanning units (LSUs) that emit light beams modulated in accordance with input signals from a controller. Following exposure, developer material—usually toner particles mixed within carrier granules or liquid developers—is applied onto this latent image to develop it into visible form via electrostatic attraction between the charged areas of the photoreceptor and oppositely-charged toners. The developed image is then transferred from the drum surface either directly onto print media (e.g., paper) in direct transfer systems or firstly onto an intermediate belt/drum before being secondarily moved to final substrate material, ensuring efficient separation of processes which enhances overall system reliability and versatility across different printing tasks like monochrome versus color prints. Once the image is transferred successfully from photoreceptor drum surface, residual toner particles remaining on it are removed by cleaning mechanisms such as blades or brushes before recharging for subsequent cycles ensuring cleanliness essential maintaining consistent quality outputs over extended usage periods without degradation due to contamination build-up. texts.full_text: The Emergent Properties of Conspecific Attraction Can Limit a Species' Ability to Track Environmental Change. Multiple biotic, abiotic, and evolutionary constraints interact to determine a species' range. However, most species are not present in all suitable and accessible locations. Dispersal ability may explain why many species do not occupy all suitable habitat, but highly mobile species also exhibit a mismatch. Habitat selection behavior where individuals are site faithful and settle near conspecifics could create a social pressure that make a species' geographic range resistant to change. We investigated this possibility by using an individual-based model of habitat selection where habitat quality moved each year. Our model demonstrated the benefits of conspecific attraction in relatively stable environments and its detrimental influence when habitat quality shifted rapidly. These results were most apparent when adult survival increased with conspecific proximity. This phenomenon may explain why highly social and long-lived species, which have higher conspecific attraction, have a higher likelihood of exhibiting geographic resistance. The results also suggest that reducing conspecific attraction may reduce social pressure, thereby allowing species to adapt to environmental changes. These findings have implications for conservation biology, particularly for highly mobile and long-lived species, which may be less able to respond to environmental changes due to conspecific attraction.
